Report.title: Report
Report.description: Represents an environmental issue report submitted by a user, including an image and categorized details.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Report entity.

userId.description: Identifier of the anonymous user who submitted this report. (Relationship: User 1:N Report)

imageUrl.description: The public URL of the image uploaded to Firebase Storage, depicting the environmental issue.

aiCategory.description: An AI-generated category for the environmental issue, e.g., 'Plastic Waste', 'Water Pollution'. This can be populated after initial report submission.

severity.description: The severity level of the reported environmental issue, as selected by the user (e.g., 'Low', 'Medium', 'High').

timestamp.description: The timestamp when the report was created and submitted.

definition.description: User-specific collection for environmental issue reports. Each document represents a single report submitted by an anonymous user. The 'userId' in the path matches the 'userId' field within the document, providing explicit ownership for robust authorization independence and secure list operations.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the anonymous user who submitted the report.

params.name: reportId
params.description: The unique identifier for the environmental issue report.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure prioritizes security, scalability, and debuggability by leveraging path-based ownership for user-specific data. Instead of a single top-level 'reports' collection for all reports, we will structure it as '/users/{userId}/reports/{reportId}'. This approach directly addresses the core design principles:

1.  **Authorization Independence (CRITICAL) & Denormalization:** Each 'Report' document will reside in a path specific to its owner: `/users/{userId}/reports/{reportId}`. The 'Report' entity itself includes a `userId` field, which effectively denormalizes the authorization context. This means security rules can verify ownership (e.g., `request.auth.uid == userId` from the path, or `request.auth.uid == resource.data.userId`) without requiring expensive and problematic `get()` calls to parent documents or other collections. This enables atomic write operations and simplifies rule logic considerably.

2.  **QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** The path-based ownership directly supports secure `list` operations. By placing a user's reports under their specific `/users/{userId}/reports` subcollection, we can implement a simple and robust security rule: `allow list: if request.auth.uid == userId;`. This rule ensures that a user can *only* list documents within their own designated subcollection. Any attempt by a client to query or list reports from another user's subcollection will be automatically denied by the rules, thereby preventing unauthorized data exposure and upholding the Query-as-Permission principle. This avoids the common pitfall where a `list` rule on a top-level collection might inadvertently expose all documents if not handled with complex, less secure `request.query` inspections. The 'Basic Report Display' feature, requiring users to view only *their own* submitted reports, is perfectly supported by this structure.
